items.front: When are additional ATC services required?
items.back: When the work situation permits. They are not optional, but they may be precluded by traffic, workload, surveillance quality, or other higher-priority factors.

items.front: What gets first priority in controller duty order?
items.back: Separating aircraft and issuing required safety alerts.

items.front: When should automation procedures be preferred over nonautomation procedures?
items.back: When workload, communications, and equipment capabilities permit.

items.front: Does filing MEDEVAC, HOSP, or AIR EVAC on a flight plan by itself create operational priority?
items.back: No. Those entries are informational only and do not identify the flight for operational-priority handling.

items.front: When should controllers use the word "immediately"?
items.back: Only when expeditious compliance is required to avoid an imminent situation.

items.front: When may a controller stop issuing further safety alerts?
items.back: After the pilot reports that action is being taken to resolve the situation.

items.front: What should you determine first after a pilot reports an inflight equipment malfunction?
items.back: The nature of the malfunction and the extent of any special handling desired.

items.front: What does a minimum-fuel advisory mean?
items.back: The aircraft cannot accept undue delay, but it is not yet declaring an emergency or requesting priority.

items.front: What kinds of conditions must be reported under 2-1-9?
items.back: Any NAS component issue or flight condition that may adversely affect air safety.

items.front: What is the first step when an aircraft reports a ground-based NAVAID malfunction?
items.back: Request a report from a second aircraft.

items.front: Can ATC invoke or deny MARSA?
items.back: No. ATC neither invokes nor denies MARSA; it only separates MARSA operations from nonparticipating IFR traffic.

items.front: When does an FAA facility apply military procedures instead of basic FAA procedures?
items.back: When it is operated by that service, exclusively supports a designated military airport, or an LOA specifies military procedures.

items.front: Who is responsible for separation between aircraft inside a formation flight?
items.back: The flight leader and the other pilots in the formation, not ATC.

items.front: Before issuing a control instruction that will affect another controller's airspace, what must be done?
items.back: Ensure all required coordination has been completed with every affected controller unless an LOA or facility directive says otherwise.

items.front: When may control be transferred by radar handoff instead of a fix, time, or altitude?
items.back: When the radar handoff and frequency change are complete and an LOA or facility directive authorizes that type of transfer.

items.front: Before clearing an aircraft into a surface area controlled by a nonapproach tower, what is required?
items.back: Coordinate with the appropriate tower unless an LOA says otherwise.

items.front: When should radio communications normally be transferred?
items.back: Before the aircraft enters the receiving controller's area of jurisdiction, unless otherwise coordinated or specified.

items.front: What does "STAND BY" mean in response to an operational request?
items.back: The request has been acknowledged, but it is neither approved nor denied yet.

items.front: For which IFR aircraft must wake turbulence separation continue to touchdown?
items.back: All IFR aircraft that are not making a visual approach or maintaining visual separation.

items.front: What must a wake turbulence cautionary advisory include?
items.back: The traffic's position, altitude if known, and direction of flight.

items.front: When must traffic advisories be issued?
items.back: When, in your judgment, proximity may drop below applicable separation minima, or otherwise warrants it where no minima apply.

items.front: How long should pilot-reported or tower-observed UAS advisories continue?
items.back: For at least 15 minutes after the last report to potentially affected aircraft.

items.front: How long should bird-activity advisories normally continue after a report?
items.back: At least 15 minutes, unless later observation or reports show the birds are no longer a factor.

items.front: What governs transfer of position responsibility between specialists?
items.back: The Standard Operating Practice for Transfer of Position Responsibility and applicable facility directives.

items.front: When is a wheels-down check required?
items.back: On each USA or USN approach unless the pilot has already reported wheels down for that approach.

items.front: What kinds of conditions must be brought to the supervisor or CIC?
items.back: Conditions affecting sector or position operations, including weather, equipment status, overload, emergencies, special operations, and suspicious activity.

items.front: When should a possible pilot deviation be reported to the pilot?
items.back: Workload permitting, when it appears the pilot's actions constitute a deviation.

items.front: What must ATC not do when a crew says it is responding to a TCAS RA?
items.back: Do not issue control instructions contrary to the RA the crew says it is executing.

items.front: What phrase must be used in verbal coordination involving a non-RVSM aircraft in RVSM airspace?
items.back: "Negative RVSM."

items.front: When does ATC responsibility for approved separation resume after a TAWS response?
items.back: When assigned altitude is regained, or the crew reports the maneuver complete or an alternate clearance flown and approved separation is reestablished.

items.front: What must happen after a report of possible human trafficking?
items.back: Notify the supervisor or CIC. These reports may be referred to as Blue Lightning events.
